retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Infectiosum -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common and classic presentation of erythema infectiosum is a mild febrile illness with a rash. Initial symptoms of infection can include fever, malaise, myalgias, diarrhea, vomiting, and headache. After initial viremia, the classic erythematous malar rash involving the cheeks with surrounding oral pallor develops. This rash does not appear early in the disease process. The lesion is classically described as a “slapped-cheek rash” and may be the only clinical diagnostic finding (see Image . Fifth Disease, Slapped-Cheek Rash). This facial rash lasts 4 to 5 days. By the time the facial rash develops, the patient usually feels well, and the viremia has resolved. This rash is thought to be immune-mediated (see Image . Fifth Disease, Reticular Facial Rash).

retrieved_docs.title: Parvoviruses -- History and Physical -- Erythema Infectiosum
retrieved_docs.content: The prodrome of symptoms begins during viremia, where patients experience a headache, myalgias, and fever. The exanthem develops one to one and one-half weeks later. It is important to know that the patient is not contagious once the eruption occurs. At this time, the patient will have a “slapped cheek” appearance, described as erythema of the cheeks, sparing the central face. This finding is the most recognizable for this disease. Adults may not have the facial rash with this infection. Later, a maculopapular eruption occurs that looks “lacy” and reticular and favors the extremities but occurs on the trunk to a lesser extent. The eruption can last anywhere from one to three weeks. The rash can worsen when exposed to heat or sunlight. Arthritis of the small joints may occur, namely the hands, feet, and knees; however, this is more common in adults. It is especially common in women and is rare in children, where it occurs in only 10%. [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Parvoviruses -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of erythema infectiosum includes scarlet fever, enteroviral infection, rubella, Still disease, erysipelas of the face, contact dermatitis, Kawasaki disease, roseola infantum, measles, and drug eruption. The differential diagnosis of PPGSS includes meningococcal infection, erythema multiforme, Henoch-Schonlein purpura, and chemotherapy-related painful acral erythema.

retrieved_docs.title: Rubeola (Measles)(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Measles should be distinguished from similar presenting exanthemic diseases of childhood, autoimmune processes, and adverse drug reactions. Rubella causes a rash similar to measles with head-caudal distribution, mild respiratory symptoms, and the absence of conjunctivitis. Still, it is accompanied by the presence of adenopathies—which is characteristic of this disease. Roseola is characterized by an illness beginning with a high fever, which subsides after a few days, accompanied by the appearance of a rash in the central part of the body, without the presence of Koplik points. Mononucleosis is a febrile viral disease, a characteristic course with few symptoms during childhood, contrary to what happens in more advanced ages. Mononucleosis manifests itself by pharyngeal compromise, polyadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly, and the rash can have different forms of presentation. In Kawasaki disease, there is an ocular compromise with the presence of conjunctivitis without exudate, and the respiratory compromise is not part of this pathology. Group A Streptococcus (particularly scarlet fever) may present with a similar rash (a coarse, sandpaper-like, blanching, erythematous) to measles in association with pharyngitis. [5] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Multiforme -- History and Physical -- Prodrome Features
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with erythema multiforme minor, prodromal symptoms are usually nonspecific, absent, or mild. [19] Patients may report fatigue, malaise, or upper respiratory tract infection. The onset of a rash usually occurs within 3 days, with centripetal spreading. In erythema multiforme major, patients have more marked symptoms, such as moderate fever, generalized aches, cough, sore throat, chest pain, vomiting, and diarrhea. These symptoms may classically persist for 2 weeks before the cutaneous lesions appear. Cough and respiratory symptoms can be seen in patients with erythema multiforme related to Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of reported AGEP cases result from adverse drug reactions, with several confirmed through patch testing. Systemic antibiotics are the most frequently implicated, particularly β-lactams and macrolides. Other reported triggers include hydroxychloroquine, antifungal, antiviral, and antiparasitic agents, as well as antineoplastic and antirheumatic drugs, analgesics, anticonvulsants, and intravenous contrast media. Corticosteroids have also been identified as causative agents in rare cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Koplik Spots -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Reports indicate that Koplik spots appear in 60% to 70% of persons suffering from measles. [5] The height of the eruption takes place when the skin eruption appears and starts spreading. As the skin rash progresses, the spot on the mucosa loses its characteristic appearance and becomes discrete, resulting in a diffuse red background with numerous bluish-white specks randomly scattered on its surface. Ultimately, after a few days, the mucosa reverts to its normal appearance. Differential diagnosis: This includes Fordyce granules (lack a bright red background), aphthous ulcers (painful and are less numerous), and parvovirus B19 infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Multiforme -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction with cutaneous and mucosal involvement. Clinically, erythema multiforme is characterized by macular, papular, bullous, or urticated lesions with a distinctive pattern of "target lesions" distributed primarily across extremities. Mucosal surfaces, including ocular, oral, and genital mucosa, may also be involved. Significant mucosal involvement differentiates erythema multiforme major from multiforme minor. Erythema multiforme is a self-limiting condition, usually resolving without significant complications, and is now classed as separate from Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis. Only a limited number of cases become persistent. Erythema multiforme's etiologies are variable and numerous, and its clinical course is generally favorable. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Pustules desquamate in a collarette-like pattern during the following 1 to 2 weeks. Additional findings may include lymphadenopathy or atypical features such as purpura, vesicles, bullae, or involvement localized to the face, neck, and chest. Historical clues supporting AGEP include rapid onset, usually within 10 days or less of exposure to the causative drug, and resolution within approximately 15 days after discontinuation. The time from onset to resolution may be prolonged in certain cases, such as AGEP induced by hydroxychloroquine or terbinafine. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Malar Rash -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of a malar rash is based on the causative condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Multiforme -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction affecting both the skin and mucous membranes, characterized by the appearance of macular, papular, or bullous lesions that often form distinct "target lesions," primarily found on the extremities. Mucosal involvement, including the eyes, mouth, or genitals, is seen more frequently in erythema multiforme major compared to the minor form. Erythema multiforme is most often triggered by infections, particularly herpes simplex virus, but can also be caused by medications, autoimmune disorders, and occasionally vaccines. In more severe cases, erythema multiforme can cause significant pain and require hospitalization, especially when mucosal lesions or dehydration occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs are most frequently implicated in AGEP, as immune-mediated reactions to drugs are more consistent and reproducible than responses to other reported triggers. Early recognition and withdrawal of the causative drug remain the most effective strategies to hasten resolution and prevent complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Infectiosum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Erythema infectiosum, also known as 5th disease, is a common viral exanthem primarily affecting children. The disorder is 1 of the 6 classic viral rash illnesses of childhood. [1] This febrile disease typically occurs in children aged 5 to 15 years, though adults may also be affected, albeit less frequently (see Image . Fifth Disease, Arm Rash). The causative agent is human parvovirus B19, which is also implicated in conditions such as aplastic anemia, polyarthropathy, and hydrops fetalis. The infection characteristically progresses through 3 distinct cutaneous phases. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Evaluation -- Maculopapular Drug Eruptions
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of maculopapular drug eruptions is primarily clinical and is based on the patient's history and physical examination. A skin biopsy does not reliably differentiate these eruptions from rashes associated with viral exanthems and is thus not helpful in confirming the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Differential Diagnosis -- Maculopapular Drug Eruptions
retrieved_docs.content: Viral infections such as measles, rubella, HHV-6, HHV-7, and HIV Bacterial infections such as M. pneumoniae, group A Streptococcus , and Treponema pallidum Acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus Juvenile idiopathic arthritis Adult-onset Still disease

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases arise as adverse drug reactions, although infectious insults and physical triggers have also been implicated. While often self-limited, AGEP can result in systemic complications. Identification and discontinuation of the offending agent remain the cornerstone of therapy. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
retrieved_docs.content: Overige gespecificeerde aandoeningen verband houdend met zwangerschap, a category denoting other specified conditions related to pregnancy, is mapped to اضطراب كلوي أثناء الحمل, indicating a kidney disorder that can occur during gestation, and is also mapped to طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال, describing a multiform rash that may appear in pregnant individuals. Recognizing that these renal and dermatologic complications are encompassed within the broader classification of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions helps clinicians identify and manage such manifestations effectively during patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other specified conditions related to pregnancy” (Overige gespecificeerde aandoeningen verband houdend met zwangerschap) comprises several distinct pregnancy‑associated disorders. It is mapped to renal disorders occurring during pregnancy, including an unspecified renal disease in pregnancy without accompanying hypertension (مرض كلوي غير محدد أثناء الحمل، دون وجود ارتفاع في ضغط الدم), as well as a multiform rash of pregnancy (طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال). Grouping these renal and dermatologic complications under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions helps clinicians recognize, code, and manage such presentations in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Overige gespecificeerde aandoeningen verband houdend met zwangerschap” (Other specified conditions related to pregnancy) is used to encompass several distinct pregnancy‑associated health issues. It is mapped to “التهاب الأعصاب الطرفية أثناء الحمل” (peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy), to the combined description “zwangerschap; vermoeidheid” (pregnancy; fatigue), and to “طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال” (multiform rash of pregnancy). These mappings indicate that this category includes neurological, constitutional, and dermatologic manifestations that can occur in the context of pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2869
retrieved_docs.content: الشعور بالضعف is clinically associated with فقر الدم, indicating that patients with anemia commonly experience a sensation of weakness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: their broader gram‑negative coverage. Dapsone is a member of the drug class used for the treatment of leprosy and is also employed in managing herpetic skin inflammation, illustrating its utility in both chronic bacterial infection and certain viral‑induced dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose veins of the lower extremity are commonly linked to the development of قَرحَة (skin ulcer), indicating that ulcerative lesions represent a typical morphological manifestation in patients with these venous disorders. This association highlights the importance of monitoring for ulcer formation when managing lower‑extremity varicose veins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications, not classified elsewhere (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) serve as an overarching category that encompasses a more specific subgroup labeled maternal pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم). Within this subgroup, several distinct conditions are identified as child entities: mild hyperemesis gravidarum (قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة), German measles occurring during pregnancy (الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل), and anemia that develops during pregnancy (فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل). These specific complications are all classified under the broader, unclassified maternal pregnancy complications category, highlighting their relevance as particular manifestations of maternal health issues that do not fit into other defined categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2876
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that encompasses several specific conditions. It is classified as including الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth) and التهاب الأعصاب الطرفية أثناء الحمل (peripheral neuritis during pregnancy). Additionally, this category has a child condition identified as طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). Together, these entities represent distinct but related complications that can occur during pregnancy, highlighting the breadth of maternal health issues that fall under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy.
